fr.name: Transferring and Mainstreaming Ecosystem Approach to Change the way Marine Protected Areas manage Climate Change and biodiversity Restoration
fr.description: Le bassin Méditerranée est considéré comme un haut lieu du changement climatique (CC), dont les effets se superposent à d’autres changements environnementaux causés par l’activité humaine comme la surpêche, la perte d'habitat et la pollution. Tout cela affecte les écosystèmes marins méditerranéens et les personnes dont les moyens de subsistance en dépendent. Afin de contribuer à inverser cette tendance à la dégradation, MPA4Change cherche à renforcer l'efficacité des aires marines protégées (AMP) et de la restauration comme solutions fondées sur la nature clef pour l'adaptation au changement climatique en déployant une approche multi-niveaux, allant de l’action locale des AMP aux stratégies méditerranéennes, afin d'assurer leur durabilité sur le long terme. Pour ce faire, MPA4Change travaillera sur 3 axes principaux : 1) promouvoir et affiner les outils préalablement testés d'évaluation des risques, de suivi, de partage des données, de restauration, de science citoyenne, d'approches participatives et de définition de plans d'action pour l’adaptation, de communication ; 2) encourager le dialogue politique et la coordination entre stratégies européennes, méditerranéennes et mondiales de PSM/GIZC comme la CGPM/FAO, le PAM/PNUE, CAR/ASP, UpM ; et 3) établir une équipe d'experts pour rendre pleinement opérationnelle la mise en œuvre des politiques et outils d’adaptation et de mitigation du CC et en assurer la viabilité à long terme. Le résultat final attendu de MPA4Change est le renforcement de la résilience des zones côtières méditerranéennes afin de garantir la conservation et la valorisation durable des écosystèmes marins. Pleinement aligné sur les défis du SO2.4 d'Interreg Euro-MED, MPA4Change augmentera la capacité d'adaptation au CC en i) renforçant l'efficacité des AMP et de la restauration, ii) améliorant la coordination et la coopération des parties prenantes au-delà des frontières et iii) accroissant la sensibilisation au CC et la culture environnementale.
fr.expected_achievements:
Improve the effectiveness of MPAs management plans, hence their resilience to CC, through the fine-tuning and transfer of validated tools and data sharing
Foment the integration of the toolkits to face CC and main recommendations into effective public policies in line with the EU/MED strategies through policy advocacy, synergies and liaising
Enhance and reinforce the operationalisation of adaptation action plans and restoration to face CC in MPA networks by creating a Roster of experts and ensure its long-term sustainability

en.name: Enhancing Marine Protected Areas as Nature Based Solutions for adaptation to climate change: from local actions to Mediterranean basin strategy
en.description: The Mediterranean Sea is considered a hot spot for climate change. The impacts of climate change are interacting with other environmental changes caused by human activities (e.g. overfishing, habitat loss and pollution). As a result, Mediterranean marine ecosystems and human livelihoods that rely on them are being affected. To contribute to reverse this severe degradation trend, MPA4Change will look to enhance the effectiveness of Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) and restoration as key Nature Based Solution for climate change adaptation by deploying a multi-level approach, from local MPA actions to Mediterranean strategies, in view to ensure its long-term sustainability and capacity. To do so MPA4Change will work on 3 main axis: 1) promote and fine-tune consolidated tools for risk assessment, monitoring, data sharing, restoration, citizen science, participatory approaches and defining adaptation action plans, communication, 2) support the Policy dialogue and coordinate with EU, Mediterranean and Global ICZM/MSP Strategies (e.g., FAO/GFCM, UNEP/MPA, RAC/SPA, UfM) and 3) develop a Roster of experts to fully operationalise the implementation of policies and tools ensuring the long-term sustainability and capacity for adaptation and mitigation to climate change. The final expected main outcome of MPA4Change is to enhance the resilience of Mediterranean coastal areas in view to support the good environmental status and sustainable use of marine ecosystems. Fully aligned with Interreg Euro-MED specific objective 2.4’s challenges, MPA4Change will increase the capacity to adapt to climate change by i) enhancing the effectiveness of MPAs and restoration, ii) improving the coordination and cooperation of stakeholders across borders to tackle them and iii) reinforcing raise awareness and environmental culture to climate change.
en.expected_achievements:
Improve the effectiveness of MPAs management plans, hence their resilience to CC, through the fine-tuning and transfer of validated tools and data sharing
Foment the integration of the toolkits to face CC and main recommendations into effective public policies in line with the EU/MED strategies through policy advocacy, synergies and liaising
Enhance and reinforce the operationalisation of adaptation action plans and restoration to face CC in MPA networks by creating a Roster of experts and ensure its long-term sustainability
en.actual_achievements:
Since its approval at the end of 2023, the MPA4Change project has made substantial progress toward enhancing Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) as nature-based solutions for climate change adaptation. The project launched with a productive Kick-off Meeting (KoM) in January 2024 in Barcelona, where partners set the groundwork for the project and established a collaborative framework for future actions. The KoM allowed partners to clarify key project objectives and roles, responsibilities, and upcoming activities, providing a clear roadmap for the project’s implementation.

The project has focused on the fine-tuning and adaptation of existing tool-kits to support the adoption of adaptation measures in MPA face to climate change, mainly based on those developed in the previous project MPAEngage but adding as well tools from other EU projects, for wider use across Mediterranean MPAs. These tools, including those for vulnerability assessments, ecological monitoring, restoration protocols, participatory approaches, development of adaptation action plans and communication materials have been identified and optimized in collaboration with key partners. A key part of this was the development of training materials, such as manuals and video tutorials, to support end users in the independent implementation of these tools. This process ensures their adaptability to different regional contexts, strengthening their potential for broader application.

One of the highlights of this period was the starting of trainings with the support of online tools and meetings plus acapacity-building workshop held in Cyprus in May 2024, where the MPA4Change consortium transferred critical monitoring and citizen science tools to partners, including MPA managers and local authorities. The training introduced partners and local stakeholders to the use of monitoring protocols such as Local Ecological Knowledge (LEK), citizen science, species-specific protocols, and temperature monitoring. These efforts aim to equip MPAs with the tools necessary to monitor and respond to climate change impacts effectively, highlighting how these tools can be integrated into day-to-day management and long-term climate resilience strategies.

Significant progress was made in communication and outreach activities, which are essential for fostering community engagement and policy advocacy. The project launched its communication channels, including a website and social media platforms, ensuring visibility and information sharing among a wider audience. A structured communication strategy was developed to promote the project's tools and achievements, aiming to engage both local stakeholders and the broader Mediterranean region. In addition, efforts have been focused on mapping potential MPA “takers” (a list of potential MPAs and stakeholders) who can adopt the project's tools, further expanding the impact of MPA4Change across the Mediterranean. 

The project maintained continuous coordination and quality assurance through regular online meetings: a monthly “Coordination Team” meeting, and specific WP leaders meetings when needed, besides the day-to-day communication between partners. Close collaboration has allowed for the effective exchange of knowledge and the development of synergies, particularly during the training and tool adaptation processes. 

Moreover, the consortium has also initiated efforts to assess its carbon footprint in line with its sustainability goals. Using the ZeroCO2Med calculator, the project is tracking the carbon footprint of its activities, in order to measure its impact and implement mitigation strategies where necessary.

In conclusion, the MPA4Change project has successfully laid a solid foundation for achieving its objectives by refining critical tools, building capacities, enhancing communication strategies, and establishing strong cooperation among partners. 
The MPA4Change project has made significant progress during its second reporting period, focusing on scientific monitoring, capacity-building, stakeholder engagement, and policy advocacy across Mediterranean Marine Protected Areas (MPAs).  One of the key areas of advancement has been the fine-tuning of the toolkits developed to enhance the effectiveness of Nature-Based Solutions (NbS), mainly the MPAs to face the climate emergency. . The toolkits support the MPAs across the entire process ensuring that MPAs have access to standardized, science-based methodologies for climate adaptation.

During this period capacity building workshops on the fine tuned toolkits to equip MPA managers have been organized during this period: i) monitoring protocols to track ecological climate impacts (temperature monitoring, benthic health assessments, and climate fish indicator surveysIi)  vulnerability assessment methodologies, iii) GIS tools and iv) citizen science program, , This trainings enhanced the ability of MPAs implementing the tools to track climate impacts and start the design of adaptive management strategies. The project is preparing for its 2025 blended learning training program, combining online courses with practical field sessions in multiple MPAs to ensure effective knowledge transfer.

It is worth noting that the Citizen Science training program saw significant progress, engaging eight diving centers and 15 instructors in climate-sensitive fish species monitoring, particularly within Atlantic MPAs. This initiative strengthens public participation in conservation and provides MPAs with valuable long-term ecological data. Besides, a major milestone in capacity building was the Vulnerability Assessment (VA) Training, which included online sessions and an intensive in-person workshop in Faro, Portugal. Participants worked with real datasets, conducted vulnerability assessments, and refined methodologies to improve their accuracy and applicability. The integration of case studies from previous adaptation projects further supported the practical application of MPA4Change tools in real-world conservation efforts.

Beyond technical advancements, the project strengthened policy engagement and stakeholder collaboration. A key policy meeting in Faro brought together MPA managers and decision-makers to explore the practical application of MPA4Change tools in daily marine governance. The project further expanded its policy influence through collaboration with existing Mediterranean initiatives, integrating some of MPA4Change toolkits into wider networks of MPAs. Participation in international and national conferences, including the 5th International Conference on Climate Change in Zadar and the Croatian Nature Protection Managers' Conference, further reinforced knowledge exchange. A survey using Mentimeter helped assess opportunities for collaboration and future implementation strategies.

A structured policy advocacy strategy was developed, including a policy roadmap, key messages, and a comprehensive event and stakeholder database to support long-term engagement with decision-makers. Policy discussions were further strengthened through participation in high-level EU and Mediterranean conservation meetings, ensuring that MPA4Change outputs contribute to broader marine governance frameworks.
The project also prioritized public outreach and communication to maximize awareness and engagement. In addition, diving protocols and promotional materials were developed to encourage recreational divers to contribute climate monitoring data, fostering citizen science engagement in MPAs.

To ensure long-term impact and sustainability, the project advanced in the design of the “100 MPA MedAlliance”. This initiative builds on three main pillars:

-	Ready to use toolkits to guide MPAs in deploying adaptation action plans
-	Dedicated long-term assistance mechanism with a roster of experts 
-	Policy & advocacy actions to enhance the role of MPAs to effectively face the climate change crisis

Moving forward, MPA4Change will focus on consolidating knowledge, expanding the implementation of tools, and ensuring long-term policy integration for Mediterranean MPAs through the “100 MPA MedAlliance”. Through continuous collaboration and engagement, the project is well-positioned to strengthen marine conservation efforts and enhance resilience to climate change, making MPAs key actors in Nature-Based Solutions for climate adaptation across the region.

en.name: Mediterranean Network for the Promotion of Sustainability & Authenticity in Olive Oil Sector
en.description:
Despite the fact that olive oil products are the most important agri-food products in the Med Area being the backbone of socio-economic and cultural life, serious difficulties had been encountered in exploiting the huge symbolic capital and integrating environmental friendly methods into SMEs activated in olive oil sector. “OliveOilMedNet” project main objective is to improve the olive oil sector by introducing innovative and effective ways through testing & validating relevant novel and eco-friendly concepts (e.g. instruments, methods, models) on the field of “sustainability” and “authenticity”. To attain its’ objective, the “OliveOilMedNet” project brings together Public Authorities, Producers’ Associations and Research Institutions from 7 Mediterranean countries, adopting, further building and go beyond on the existing practices that have been developed in other projects, by strengthen the capacity of a) the national/regional/local authorities engaged in the policymaking in the olive oil sector, and b) the olive oil companies to produce more competitive, “authentic” and “green” olive oil products, in collaboration with academic / research institutions and social society organization in the logic of 4helix, through the:
- identification and certification of the variety/botanical identity, specific organoleptic and quality characteristics, and geographical origin (authenticity) of extra virgin olive oils from different olive cultivars in the Mediterranean Area with the use of a classification model capable for olive cultivar/geographical prediction,
- development of an innovative network of stakeholders throughout the olive oil chain for the promotion of sustainable production and consumption practices to mitigate climate change impacts on the environment, 
- introduction of eco-friendly cultivation techniques, sustainable technology solutions in olive groves and olive oil mills, and the use of socio-economic and environmental evaluation tools and methodologies.
en.expected_achievements:
Test and adopt procedures and methodologies in order to promote the sustainability, authenticity and eco-friendly cultivation and production practices in the olive oil sector.
Certification of the geographical / botanical identity of extra virgin olive oils and integration of eco-friendly cultivation and production techniques and their socio-economic and environmental evaluation.
Strengthen the capacity of the national/regional/local authorities engaged in the policymaking in the olive oil sector and the olive oil companies to produce more competitive, “authentic” and “green” olive oil products.
en.actual_achievements:
WP1
LP prepared the Detailed Plan of the Act. 1.1 accompanied with a "Data Collection Template" and presented it to the partnership during the Kick-off Meeting. Until 06.2024 LP, PP4, PP5, PP8, PP9 and PP10 worked on the filling of the “Data Collection Template” and made all relevant contacts with various stakeholders to ensure that the data collected is representative of the national reality. Moreover, specific information has been collected about relevant instruments, strategies and solutions already developed/ implemented in the framework of previous projects.  
Furthermore,  PP2 and PP10 prepared the Detailed Plan of the Act. 1.2. and 2 specific methods for the fruit and physicochemical characterisation of olive oils. These two methodology guidelines or protocols are presented to all partners in the Kick-off Meeting and will be applied in the next olive campaign, 2024/25. PP9 completed the reception and setup of the NIRS equipment (FOSS, DS2500L, Serial no. 91879788) has been carried out, necessary for the physical-chemical characterization of the olive oils produced.   
WP3
In the framework of Act. 3.1, LP organised an online Meeting for the establishment the Steering Committee of OliveOilMedNet project on  08/02/2024.  All partners participated and the Project Steering Committee (PSC) and the  Scientific Advisory Board (SAB) were appointed. Also the tasks of Project Steering Committee was determined and the following persons were appointed: PC, FM, CM, RAC, CFC, DPO and ALP.
The Kick-off Meeting of the project was organised (hybrid) by LP on 13 & 14/05/2024 in the held of the Chamber. All partners participated and the work plan was agreed. Activity leaders presented the detail plan of the activities.
LP prepared the Risk Management & Quality Assurance Plan which describe specific processes,  roles/responsibilities to ensure the successful implementation of the project. 
Furthermore, PP9 selected 18 farmers and 2 olive mills in Andalusia to participate in the test phases of the project and prepared a seminar on harvesting and production techniques of early olive oils.
The project web site is ready and feeded with information photographes and news about the project. Also social media are created. Press releases were published in project website and the institutional web site. Communication guidelines are followed. 
On 13.05.2024 an info day was organised by LP in the held of the Chamber.  In this event LP and PP2 after the project presentation discussed with SMEs highlighting the benefits that they will be emerged from the implementation of OliveOilMedNet project. In this event olive farmers, olive oil producers and a representative from Region of Epirus participated informed about the project and its objectives.   
Moreover, a WhatsApp network has been established between farmers and olive oil millers selected in Spain by the University of Jaen. 
Finally, in the framework of Act. 3.3, LP, PP7 and PP9 participated in the event "SHAPING SUSTAINABLE INNOVATION IN THE MEDITERRANEAN" organised by ISE in Marseille on 18 and 19 May 2024, in World Trade Center. In more detail Preveza Chamber participated in the Launch of the Community of Practice of the Innovative Sustainable Economy Mission and in the 1st edition of the MED Innovation Day.
Based on the above, the implementation of the project had started smoothly and on time contributing to the promotion of sustainability, authenticity and eco-friendly cultivation and production concepts in the olive oil sector, which is the main aim of WP1.
Finally, all the above tasks has been designed and implemented with the active participation of all transnational project’s partners.


The project's specific objectives are partly achieved. We are already testing methodologies of authenticity in a great nummber of producers in all participating countries. Moreover, the preliminary framework for the organisation of the Capacity building activities was dicusssed outlining key objectives and planned actions. The development of the e-network platform has started
The preliminary study has been finilised, with evaluated data, from the participating countries (Greece, Italy, France, Cyprus, Albania, Portugal and Spain), regarding the folowing subjects:
-	Olive Oil Description / Current situation 
-	Prospects & Trends of the Olive – Oil Sector
-	Identified instruments, strategies, tools for the authenticity certification of olive oils
-	Eco-friendly cultivation techniques and production processes in the olivewas completed with the contribution  of partners that  completed the Data collection template with information concerning their country/region. 
The activity 1.2 is under finilisation. Two methodologies are developing concerning a)the identification and certification of geographical origin  of extra virgin olive oils,b) the use eco-friendly cultivation techniques and sustainable technological solutions and the use of the socio-economic and envir. evaluation tools.
All PPs invited olive oil producers to participate in the pilot activity of the project. The specialized staff of every partner, with knowledge in the field, contacted the interested olive oil producers and confirmed their eligibility. Moreover all PPs started collecting olive oil samples. The collected samples were recorded,   stored and sent to the laboratory for analyses.The sample collection of all PPs concerned specific local varieties. The data base with the results of chemical and organoleptic analysis is developed. 
The preliminary framework for the organisation of the Capacity building activities was dicusssed outlining key objectives and planned actions.The development of the e-network platform has started. 
The project web site and project’ s social media were feeded with information photographs and news about the project. Press releases were published. All partners disseminate the project in their communication channels. Finally, the project video was developed and the 4 pages leaflet was designed. Also yhe project was disseminated in a national chanel and an info day was organised to informed stakeholders about the project.
Partnership participate in ISE CoP gathering, also an interview was given and the the video presenting the Cluster of Agri-food System was developed. 
Partnership make efforts to reduce the emmission carbon footprint. 
Regarding the certification of the authenticity of extra virgin olive oil (act 1.3), all PPs selected the appropriate beneficiaries (farmers and mill owners). Olive oil samples from selected local monovarietal extra virgin olive oils, collected during the two sampling periods of the first year (2024–2025) in the framework of the test phase, were recorded, stored, and transferred for laboratory analysis. These samples contribute both to the Olive Oil Composition Data Set and to the development of the  authenticity model.
LP and P8 sent samples to PP2, PP4 sent samples to P9, and P5 sent samples to P6. PP2, PP6, PP9, and PP10, which have the necessary laboratories, along with P7 through a subcontractor, carried out the chemical and organoleptic analyses. Only at P9, the sensory evaluation of oils from this first campaign is still pending.
The results of these analyses were transmitted to P2 for further integration into the project’s digital outputs. This activity supports the identification of authentic extra virgin olive oils.
Concerning sustainable practices (act 1.4), PP9 requested that all PPs select 10 beneficiaries and collect data related to farming and processing techniques. Most PPs gathered and submitted detailed information on cultivation methods, production techniques, and marketing practices of their selected beneficiaries. The completed data, structured in an Excel file, was then submitted to PP9 for assessment.
The results of the above analyses of monovarietal extra virgin olive oil samples, regarding authenticity, have been compiled into a comprehensive dataset that will serve as the foundation for the online Authentic Olive Data Bank (D.2.1.1).
The content of the Sustainable Olive Oil Growing & Production Methodology Handbook (D.2.2.1) was determined by P9 and agreed upon by the PPs, with some PPs contributing to the preparation of specific chapters.
PP4 organized the 3rd Steering Committee Meeting, held on 3–4 April 2025 in Alentejo, Portugal. PPs met to review project progress and take decisions.
In addition, the 1st Hybrid Peer Learning Event and the 1st Demonstration Event were (act 3.1) held on 3 and 4 April respectively, also in Alentejo. Both events focused on olive oil authenticity. PPs actively engaged in discussions and knowledge exchange during these events. The Demonstration Event attracted a large number of producers and stakeholders from 10 different countries, many of whom were also participating in the 14th International Extra Virgin Olive Oil Competition – Ovibeja Award 2025, one of the most prestigious international olive oil competitions in the world.
Regarding the E-Training (act 3.1) component, PP7 provided guidelines for the development of seminar materials. The proposed topics were reviewed and validated by the consortium, laying the foundation for the upcoming digital learning modules.
Regarding D3.2.1 e networking Platform, P5 made a presentation in the 3rd Project Meeting and the content was agreed. 
PP5 prepared the action plan (act 3.2) for the establishment of the OliveOilMedNet. P7 contributed to drafting the Action Plan and took full responsibility for preparing the Memorandum of Understanding (MoU), which will serve as a key framework for future cooperation within the Med Olive Oil Network. Moreover, LP and P8 contributed to the finalisation of both the Action Plan and the MoU to ensure the long-term sustainability of the Network. Furthermore, PPs developed contacts with various entities in the olive oil sector to attract them to join the Network (Activity 3.2). Several expressions of interest were received, supporting the establishment of the Network. In particular, LP, P2, P4, P7, P8, and P10 contributed to an Excel database of stakeholders, which includes declarations of interest from parties wishing to join the project’s MoU.
On communication, LP supported all project partners in adhering to Euro-MED visibility rules and compiled the 2024 communication reports and data. As part of dissemination, LP uploaded materials to the project’s website and social media platforms, sharing information, photos, and event updates. Press releases were published, and all partners promoted the project through their respective communication channels. A highlight was PP9’s project presentation at EXPOLIVA 2025.
Finally, the project was represented in several events under the Innovative Sustainable Economy (ISE) Mission, including the 3rd ISE Community of Practice (Sarajevo, April 2025) and several online meetings of the ISE Mentoring & Twinning Programme and Communication Network. These engagements strengthened partnerships and increased the project’s visibility.
The PPs also participated in the carbon footprint assessment activity, coordinated by Partner 09 (University of Jaén), contributing data and expertise to support the environmental performance analysis of olive oil production.

fr.name: Réseau méditerranéen pour la promotion de la durabilité et de l'authenticité dans le secteur de l'huile d'olive
fr.description:
Malgré le fait que les produits de l'huile d'olive soient les produits agroalimentaires les plus importants dans l'Espace Med, de difficultés ont été rencontrées dans l'exploitation de capital symbolique et l'intégration de méthodes respectueuses de l'environnement dans les PME actives du secteur de l'huile d'olive. L'objectif principal du "OliveOilMedNet" est d'améliorer le secteur de l'huile d'olive par l’introduction de moyens en testant et en validant des concepts nouveaux et respectueux de l'environnement dans le domaine de la "durabilité" et de "l'authenticité". Pour atteindre son objectif, « OliveOilMedNet » rassemble les autorités publiques, les associations de producteurs et les institutions de recherche de 7 pays méd, adoptant, développant et dépassant les pratiques existantes (d'autres projets), par le renforcement de la capacité a) des autorités engagées dans l'élaboration des politiques dans le secteur de l'huile d'olive, et b) des sociétés oléicoles pour produire des produits d'huile d'olive plus compétitifs, «authentiques» et «verts», en collaboration avec des institutions universitaires / de recherche et l'organisation sociale et sociétale dans la logique de 4helix, à travers :
- l’identification et la certification de l'identité variétale/botanique, des caractéristiques organoleptiques et qualitatives spécifiques et de l'origine géographique (authenticité) des huiles d'olive extra vierges de différents cultivars d'olive dans la région méd à l'aide d'un modèle de classification,
- le développement d'un réseau innovant d'acteurs tout à la chaîne de l'huile d'olive pour la promotion de pratiques de production et de consommation durables afin d'atténuer les impacts du changement climatique,
- l’introduction de techniques de culture respectueuses de l'environnement, de solutions technologiques durables dans les oliveraies et les moulins à huile d'olive, et l’utilisation d'outils et de méthodologies d'évaluation socio-économique et environnementale.

en.name: COOL NOONS
en.description:
Summer 2022: Lisbon 43°, Athens 45°, Syracuse 48°....
All the experts agree. Summer 2022 has given us a foretaste of what the future climate will be like in the Mediterranean, a climate in which extremes will become more pronounced and heat waves will continue to multiply. Under these conditions, who will dare to visit Mediterranean cities during the months of July and August? Who will still be walking around Marseille or Bologna in the middle of summer? 
The Cool Noons project aims to address the issue of urban tourism in Mediterranean cities, dramatically exposed to the negative effects of climate change and in particular to heat waves. . How to renew the urban tourism offer by adapting it to a context of extreme and rapid changes? How to improve the comfort of cities in summer for the benefit of tourists and inhabitants? How can we ensure the sustainability of an economic activity as fundamental as tourism in Mediterranean countries?
In Cool Noons project, five pilot Mediterranean cities will test solutions to improve the offer of activities for both tourists and inhabitants during the hottest hours of the day. Some of the solutions to be tested have already demonstrated their effectiveness (in contexts not linked with tourism) such as revegetation, water equipment or light-colored urban surfaces. Other new solutions will emerge from the creativity of the end "users" of the cities, i.e. the residents and tourists. 
The solutions tested will also be cross evaluated to clarify their potential for replication in the Mediterranean region.
Cool Noons is an innovative project for the way it addresses an urgent and underestimated issue such as the evolution of urban tourism towards a more sustainable model. It is also innovative for the tools and methods used, which combine scientific accuracy, pragmatism and creativity, thanks to the diversity of the partners involved.
en.expected_achievements:
To improve the knowledge of project partners on the behavior/needs of visitors in order to identify the most relevant  solutions through a co-design process involving final users  (citizen inhabitants and visitors) 



Increase the resilience and the attractiveness of urban areas for tourists and inhabitants by testing innovative solutions to adapt to climate change

Ensuring the production of efficient, easily transferable and ready-to-use tools and methodologies to influence public policies on urban tourism.
en.actual_achievements:
During the first six months of COOL NOONS, the consortium has focused its efforts on setting up the collaborative framework, the key tools to effectively manage and communicate about the project, and the main frameworks to launch the first activities in the pilot cities (Imola, Lisbon, Marseille, Dubrovnik and Budva). 

For what concerns the project’s management, the lead partner AVITEM has set up several tools. A project Vademecum has been developed with the purpose of summarizing all the key elements related to the project. Several factsheets related to the financial management of the project have also been made available to the partners. An additional document detailing the project management bodies and the rules of procedure of the Steering Committee has also been produced by AVITEM and validated by all partners. A Basecamp space dedicated to COOL NOONS has been set up and is going to be used as the main online repository for the working documents and key productions. A timeline of the COOL NOONS activities has been produced by AVITEM and is used to monitor the progress of all the activities. 

The consortium has also established a framework to regularly meet and update each other about the progress on the activities. It has been decided that, besides the formal Steering Committee meetings (two per year, one in-person and one online), there will be monthly online meetings every third Tuesday of each month. These meetings are a very effective way to keep each other informed about the progress of the activities and discuss any potential difficulties. 
A first in-person Steering Committee meeting has been held in Marseille on the 25th March, followed by a training session on Design Thinking on the 26th and 27th March, and a data collection workshop on the 27th March. This meeting has allowed to create a positive collaboration dynamic among the partnership, and to officially kick-off the first activities of the project, and especially the first testing which will take place during summer 2024.

The communication of COOL NOONS has also been launched during this first reporting period. The Communication strategy (Deliverable 2.1.1) has been drafted by Imola Faenza Tourism Company (IFTC) with the support of AVITEM and with input from all partners. It has been validated by the JS and is now used as the main framework to carry out the communication activities of the project. The project’s website has been set up and is now online: it acts as our main online showcase space where anyone can find the key information and news about the project. IFTC has also set up the social media channels for the project: Facebook, Instagram and LinkedIn. The project also has a YouTube channel. Partners have also started communicating about COOL NOONS on their own online channels. 

Following the data collection workshop in Marseille on the 27th March, the University of Coimbra has developed a methodological framework (Del. 1.1.1) to help the pilot partners to collect the data in their territories. The American College of Greece has also drafted a cross-cultural evaluation methodology (Del. 1.1.2) which complements this methodological framework. 

AVITEM has conducted a training session on Design Thinking and has produced a Design Thinking methodology (Del. 1.1.3), aimed at helping the pilot partners to organise the collective intelligence workshops with local citizens and stakeholders, to co-design solutions to improve the experience of visiting their city (for tourists and residents) during the hottest hours of the day.

The municipality of Lisbon has provided instructions and insights to help pilot partners to map their territories, with the aim to identify the Cool Noons paths: itineraries in their cities that connect fresh spots in the city and/or spaces where tourists and residents can find relief from the heat. IFTC has provided instructions and a template for the promotion tools for these paths. Based on this information, and on the testing instructions provided by the Budva municipality, partners are now ready to identify and test their Cool Noons paths. 

DURA Dubrovnik has provided instructions to partners for the composition of local committees that will follow the project’s activities in the pilot cities. These committees will involve local tourism stakeholders, municipality staff, residents’ representatives, elected officials, technical officers, etc. They will select the ideas generated in the collective intelligence workshops to be implemented in 2025 through the investments.

The Metropolitan city of Bologna has shared instructions and tools for the carbon footprint monitoring.
AVITEM has started engaging with the horizontal projects, by meeting with the Community4Tourism Angels from the University of Thessaly, attending the C4T meeting in Rome and presenting the project at the Dialogue4Tourism meeting in Marseille.
This past year has been marked by significant global challenges—climate disasters, conflicts, and both sorrow and hope across the world. Summer 2024 was the hottest ever recorded on Earth, while Spain endured its worst flooding in decades. These events have underscored the critical need for the tourism sector to adapt to climate change.

The Cool Noons project will test solutions for improving urban tourism comfort during the hottest hours of the day, and therefore aims at adapting Mediterranean urban tourism to climate changes. 

During the first months, the consortium laid the foundation of the project. 
In April, the project’s kick-off meeting in Marseille established the governance structure and the operational framework of the consortium. 

1/ Specific objective: to improve the knowledge of project partners on the behavior/needs of visitors and to expand the range of possible solutions through a co-design process involving citizen

Started in January 2024, Cool Noons operates using an innovative methodology that integrates participative and evaluative processes. 
The University of Coimbra developed a robust methodological framework, instructing pilot cities to gather data across three assessments: 1) weather data, 2) tourist attractiveness, and 3) experience enhancement (for both residents and tourists).
ACG-RC contributed by creating a cross-cultural data collection methodology, ensuring the project’s adaptability for future implementation in new cities.

In Summer, each pilot city carried out extensive data collection, guided by these frameworks. The methodologies were adapted to the specific context and available tools. For instance, Lisbon’s Municipality and DURA used thermal cameras and QR codes, Marseille’s Municipality conducted surveys via social media, while Budva’s Municipality and Bologna’s Metropolis conducted in-person surveys. 

Once collected, the data was shared with the University of Coimbra and the American College of Greece as a foundation to refine project strategies and ensure a common analytical approach. 

One of the key success factors for the Cool Noons project concerns its participative approach. 
During the project’s kick-off meeting in April, a design thinking workshop was organized. It aimed at expanding the toolkit of each pilot city for a better co-design approach. 

During Autumn, participatory workshops occurred for most pilot sites with tailor-made methodologies. 

For example, in Imola, participatory urban walks on the paths were organized with students in Environmental Management, in Accessible and Sustainable Tourism and in Construction, Environment, and Territory. Citizens and NGOs were also involved and the workshops.

Another example: Lisbon mixed tools, including participative walks and creation of Lego models to represent a set of selected solutions: afforestation, with native species and those adapted to the ecosystem; soil support and greater carbon sequestration; planting trees along the trail to increase shade; installation of a pergola with a green roof; requalification of the existing information panel; and resurfacing of the parking area to ensure a high level of infiltration.

In Marseille, the lead of those citizens workshops was given to the Citizen Participation department, specialized in organizing those participative democratic processes. 

2/ Specific objective: To increase the resilience and the attractiveness of urban areas for tourist and inhabitants testing innovative solutions to adapt to climate change

Our pilot cities (Lisbon, Marseille, Budva, Dubrovnik, Imola) seek to promote new tourist attractions in less frequented areas and develop solutions to urban heat in the most visited places.
In Spring, the pilot cities focused on characterizing tourist points of interest, identifying cool spots (such as green areas and water points) and measuring thermal/weather data. The routes were mapped and further on, they were promoted thanks to digital and printed maps (printed in a few thousand copies for each site) and supported by the Local Tourism Boards.

In addition, the co-conceived solutions proposed will contribute to the city’s resilience in the context of climate change and heat waves. In the participatory workshops, priority intervention areas were identified: wind-based solutions, digital-based solutions, water-based solutions, urban furniture-based solutions, shaded-based solutions. The solutions most suited to each location will be implemented during Spring 2025 along the Cool Noons paths.
The past few years have been marked by significant global tragedies. Summer 2024 was the hottest ever recorded on Earth, while Spain endured its worst flooding in decades. June 2025 Europe was hit by a quite early heatwave. These events have underscored the critical need for the tourism sector to adapt to climate change, to promote disaster risk prevention, resilience, taking into account eco-system based approaches.
During this reporting period, The COOL NOONS project developed in a significant progress, with a strong focus on stakeholder engagement, pilot implementation, and strategic communication. 

Project coordination
Monthly coordination meetings(every third Tuesday afternoon of the month) were held between the project team to ensure smooth implementation of the upcoming activities.
A steering committee meeting was host by Municipality of Lisbon, in Portugal, on March 31 and April 1, 2025. The event gathered project partners and associated partners, including representatives from MedCities, Lisbon Tourism Board, Municipality of Dubrovnik and the Dubrovnik’s Maritime Museum. During the meeting, two panels were organized: one about climate change adaptation to heat in each pilot city, the second one focusing on the conditions and perspectives for Mediterranean Sustainable Tourism.

Stakeholder engagement
Cool Noons operates using an innovative methodology that integrates participative and evaluative processes.
Budva organized structured focus groups with key local stakeholders - urban planners, representatives of vulnerable groups, youth organizations, local institutions- in March 2025.
In all pilot cities, key achievements include the continuation of visitor feedback along "Cool Paths" to record their experiences regarding heat perception and access to cool spaces. This ongoing data collection and analysis is guided by University of Coimbra & American College of Greece.

Pilot implementation : cool paths promotion and solutions
Specific tools have been leveraged to promote Cool Noons paths:
-	A mobile App in Budva 
-	An interactive mobile-optimized web platform in Dubrovnik (Cool Noons - Dubrovnik)
-	Official municipal webpages (for ex : in Lisbon (Percursos Ambientais) and in Marseille Projet européen "Cool Noons" : pour des parcours de fraîcheur en été | Ville de Marseille) 
-	News in local media and social media
-	Printed maps in Imola, Marseille and Lisbon 
-	Public guided visits in Lisbon on Cool Paths on 10 May, 24 May, 23 June, 25 June and photo contest in Monsanto 
-	Video in Imola Progetti europei - Imola presenta il Piano d'Azione del progetto Cool Noons: città più verdi e vivibili contro le isole di calore
-	A press conference on 25 June in Marseille
-	And a variety of promotional materials aligned with Euro-MED Program guidelines

IFTC created an online Catalogue of the solutions - Interreg Euro-MED - COOL NOONS.
In Budva, spatial and technical assessments and administrative procedures were completed for greening the corridor between Akademija Znanja and the Old Town. 
In Imola, the Action Plan for Heat-Resilient Public Spaces shows three cooling routes and twelve key places to adapt with NBS-solutions. It was presented publicly on April 1st, 2025, at the City Council Hall in Imola. 
In Marseille, 9 pergolas and 33 climbing plants and 3 fountains have been implemented alongside Cool Paths. New Cool Paths are envisioned for the next years.
In Dubrovnik, DURA initiated the development of a mobile app featuring augmented reality elements, along with the procurement of tablets and accompanying equipment for the Maritime Museum in Dubrovnik, aiming at promoting the visits of the AC museum.
In Lisbon, initial intervention was completed in March 2025. Two additional interventions of NBS are planned in the green corridors and will be noted for the next reporting period.
For any investment, a common graphic layout for panels and signs have been designed by IFTC. 
 
Replicability of the project
MCBO started working on D. 3.2.1 - Guidelines for renewal of public policies, defining the best tools and methods, an action plan and a short list of clear and "ready to use” for decision makers.
AVITEM proposed strategic perspectives and reflections: Cool Noons as a methodology and as a program for a renewal of urban planning/fabric. It will be further developed in the Transferring Strategy and Action Plan.

The project was presented in the conference “Surchauffe Urbaine: comment adapter nos centres historiques patrimoniaux?” held on March 10th in Marseille.
AVITEM participated in mission’s governance projects and contributes to the amplification strategy of Interreg Euro-Med community.

MCBO managed the carbon footprint monitoring, collecting and uploading data to the platform.

fr.name: Cool Noons
fr.description:
Eté 2022 : Lisbonne 43°, Athènes 45°, Syracuse 48°….
Tous les experts s’accordent à dire que l’été 2022 nous a offert un avant-goût de ce que le climat futur sera en Méditerranée, un climat dans lequel les extrêmes s’accentueront et les vagues de chaleurs ne cesseront de se multiplier. Dans ces conditions qui osera visiter les villes pendant les mois de juillet et août ? Qui se baladera encore en plein été à Marseille ou Bologne ? 
Cool Noons se propose de traiter la question du tourisme urbain dans les villes méditerranéennes fortement exposées aux effets néfastes du changement climatique et notamment aux vagues de chaleurs. Comment renouveler l’offre de tourisme urbain en l’adaptant à un contexte de changements aussi extrêmes que rapides ? Comment améliorer le confort des villes l’été au bénéfice des touristes et des habitants ? Comment assurer la pérennité d’une activité économique aussi structurante que le tourisme en Méditerranée?
Dans Cool Noons, cinq villes pilotes méditerranéennes de différents pays testeront des solutions pour améliore l’accueil des visiteurs pendant les heures plus chaudes de la journée. 
Des solutions déjà connues et reconnues comme efficaces telles que la végétalisation, l’aménagement des points d’eau ou les revêtements urbains de couleurs claires mais aussi des solutions nouvelles qui émergeront de la créativité des « usagers » des villes, à savoir les résidents et les touristes. 
Les solutions testées feront également l’objet d’une évaluation croisée qui permettra d’expliciter leur potentiel de réplicabilité dans le pourtour méditerranéen.
Cool Noons est un projet innovant pour la manière dont il aborde un sujet aussi urgent que sous-estimé comme l’évolution du tourisme urbain vers un modèle plus durable et pérenne. Il est également innovant pour les outils et méthodes mobilisés qui associent rigueur scientifique, pragmatisme et créativité, et ce grâce à la diversité des partenaires impliqués.

To improve the knowledge of project partners on the behavior/needs of visitors in order to identify the most relevant  solutions through a co-design process involving final users  (citizen inhabitants and visitors) 

Increase the resilience and the attractiveness of urban areas for tourists and inhabitants by testing innovative solutions to adapt to climate change
Ensuring the production of efficient, easily transferable and ready-to-use tools and methodologies to influence public policies on urban tourism.

An additional document detailing the project management bodies and the rules of procedure of the Steering Committee has also been produced by AVITEM and validated by all partners. A Basecamp space dedicated to COOL NOONS has been set up and is going to be used as the main online repository for the working documents and key productions. A timeline of the COOL NOONS activities has been produced by AVITEM and is used to monitor the progress of all the activities. 

The consortium has also established a framework to regularly meet and update each other about the progress on the activities. It has been decided that, besides the formal Steering Committee meetings (two per year, one in-person and one online), there will be monthly online meetings every third Tuesday of each month.
