fr.name: Réseau méditerranéen de sites SIPAM pour promouvoir un tourisme agricole durable
fr.description:
Les Systèmes Ingénieux du Patrimoine Agricole Mondial (SIPAM) sont des agroécosystèmes habités par des communautés vivant dans une relation complexe avec leur territoire. Ces sites sont des systèmes résilients caractérisés par une agrobiodiversité remarquable, des savoirs traditionnels, des cultures et des paysages inestimables, gérés durablement par les agriculteurs, les éleveurs, les pêcheurs et les populations forestières de manière à contribuer à leurs moyens de subsistance et à leur sécurité alimentaire.

L'enjeu principal du projet est de renforcer le rôle du tourisme durable dans la préservation de ces incroyables sites agricoles qui font face à plusieurs menaces grâce à une stratégie de valorisation du tourisme agricole durable adaptée, améliorée et validée. Dans les sites SIPAM, une gestion délicate est nécessaire : les moyens de subsistance des sites peuvent dépendre des visiteurs venant d’ailleurs pour diversifier leurs activités économiques, mais le tourisme est un facteur de pression sur ces systèmes fragiles. La durabilité est la clé.

Le principal résultat du projet sera la création d'un réseau MED-GIAHS innovant basé sur le tourisme qui améliorera la durabilité des sites e contribuera à reconstruire et à redéfinir le tourisme d'une manière nouvelle et plus durable.

Les résultats du projet seront transférables à tous les sites MED-GIAHS, principalement la stratégie de tourisme durable et le réseau d'échange et de soutien. Les résultats attendus du projet sont une guide conjoint pour définir des plans d'action locaux, des catalogues de bonnes pratiques et du matériel de formation et d'information.

L'approche transnationale et le soutien de la FAO contribueront à résoudre les problèmes communs à tous les sites SIPAM et à atteindre les résultats escomptés du projet. À savoir, créer des moyens de subsistance en combinant la conservation du patrimoine, la tradition et l'innovation avec le tourisme agricole durable en tant qu'activité prometteuse pour stimuler le développement rural.
fr.expected_achievements:
To optimize the VALSIPAM strategy that will be improved thanks to the project’s activities. These will lead to a new strategy on sustainable agricultural tourism for the Mediterranean GIAHS sites as a basis for the MED GIAHS network creation. 
To improve the skills of stakeholders about sustainable tourism in agricultural heritage systems through training material, a joint definition of action plans and the study visits activities that promote agritourism in GIAHS sites. These actions will lead to the elaboration of the Strategy for Sustainable Agricultural Tourism in MED GIAHS sites
To bring together public/private key actors of GIAHS agritourism value chain in the newly created MED-GIAHS network to cooperate in their promotion as unique and sustainable tourism destinations and diversify local economic activities.

en.name: Mediterranean GIAHS sites network to promote sustainable agricultural tourism
en.description:
The Globally Important Agricultural Heritage Systems (GIAHS) are agroecosystems inhabited by communities that live in intricate relationships with their territory. These evolving sites are resilient systems characterised by remarkable agrobiodiversity, traditional knowledge, invaluable cultures, and landscapes that are sustainably managed by farmers, herders, fisherfolk, and forest people in ways that contribute to their livelihoods and food security.

The main challenge of the project is to reinforce the role of sustainable tourism in preserving these incredible agricultural sites that face several threats through the transfer of VALSIPAM project strategy that will be adapted, improved, and validated for sustainable agricultural tourism valorisation. In GIAHS sites, tourism requires particular careful management: the livelihoods of Med GIAHS sites population may rely on foreign visitors to diversify their economic activities, but tourism is a pressure factor on these fragile systems. Sustainability is the key.

The main project result will be the creation of an innovative tourism-based MED-GIAHS network that will enhance the sustainability of existing, candidate, and future MED-GIAHS. It will help rebuild and redefine tourism in a new and more sustainable way.

The project results will be transferable to all the MED-GIAHS sites, mainly the improved sustainable tourism strategy and the network for exchange and support. Expected project results for designated and candidate GIAHS sites in the Med area are joint guide for local action plans, good practices catalogues, and training and information material.

This project's transnational approach and the support of FAO would contribute to addressing common problems at all GIAHS sites and achieving the expected results. That is, creating livelihoods in GIAHS sites by combining heritage conservation, tradition and innovation in a unique way with sustainable agricultural tourism as a promising activity to boost rural development.
en.expected_achievements:
To optimize the VALSIPAM strategy that will be improved thanks to the project’s activities. These will lead to a new strategy on sustainable agricultural tourism for the Mediterranean GIAHS sites as a basis for the MED GIAHS network creation. 
To improve the skills of stakeholders about sustainable tourism in agricultural heritage systems through training material, a joint definition of action plans and the study visits activities that promote agritourism in GIAHS sites. These actions will lead to the elaboration of the Strategy for Sustainable Agricultural Tourism in MED GIAHS sites
To bring together public/private key actors of GIAHS agritourism value chain in the newly created MED-GIAHS network to cooperate in their promotion as unique and sustainable tourism destinations and diversify local economic activities.
en.actual_achievements:
During the first six months of the MED GIAHS project, the planned activities have been developed with no significant deviations from those initially planned.

Specific tasks have been carried out for the start-up, operation, control and monitoring of the project. In addition, others related to the general objective of the project, which is to develop a sustainable tourism model in the GIAHS/candidate territories, and to establish a network of GIAHS sites in the Mediterranean area, have been also developed.

The consortium launched the project activities with the Steering Committee meeting held in telematic format in March 2024 where all partners participated. They presented their entities, reasons for participating in the project and main interests and motivations for participating in MED GIAHS. The procedures, responsibilities, administrative and financial operating rules, monitoring, evaluation plans and main aspects that should govern the communication activities were also presented.

Another important achievement was the first Inter-regional seminar of the project held in March 2024. The aim was to present, discuss and clarify all aspects related to the project activities, workshops, meetings, seminars and the main expected results and products to be developed during the first semester. Likewise, the technical work teams for each partner were defined. A relevant aspect of this interregional seminar was the presentation of the strategy of the Valsipam project, financed by the SUDOE Interregional Cooperation Programme, which developed a strategy for sustainable tourism in the GIAHS territories of Southwest Europe. The way in which this strategy should be transferred, adapted and improved for the unique agricultural territories participating in the MED GIAHS project was also presented. The second important achievement of the project was also shown and explained: a joint guide for the elaboration of action plans. The partner in charge of the development of the joint training plan for the stakeholders in the different GIAHS territories informed about the objectives of this task and the stakeholder groups to be targeted by this training. The interregional seminar laid the groundwork for the first technical meeting of the project, to be held in May 2024, which would set out the procedures and common tools to address the first phase of the sustainable tourism model to be developed: the tourism analysis of the territories. Finally, the thematic community of sustainable tourism of the Euromed area was presented.

The community4tourism project held its kick-off meeting in April 2024 in person in Rome where the lead partner presented MED GIAHS. Other partners also participated in the meeting. 

During the month of May, the first technical meeting of the project was held in telematic format. This meeting defined the common tools and protocols that all partners should follow to develop the first phase of the new sustainable tourism strategy of MED GIAHS: the analysis of the tourism situation in each GIAHS territory. All partners participated in this meeting, as well as the lead partner of the project community4tourism, who explained the general objectives of the project and how MED GIAHS contributes to the creation of the sustainable tourism community and benefits from the synergies provided by this community. The lead partner presented the criteria and main characteristics of the GIAHS sites and the sustainable tourism strategies within the action plans developed in these unique territories. For its part, the partner Council of the Huerta de Valencia, responsible for the activity, presented the tools, process, and timetable for developing the analysis of the tourism situation in the GIAHS territories.

These analyses were carried out in each territory before the end of the first semester of the project, preferably in person in what was known as the 1st Local Workshop, with the participation of all the actors involved in tourism in each GIAHS/candidate territory. All partners have held their workshops. The aspects addressed were as follows: Characterisation of the demand and typology of current customers in the territories, the most relevant tourism resources in each area, tourism experiences that can add significant value to the tourism strategy to be designed and good practices that can be exportable to other territories.

With this activity, the MED GIAHS project has completed the technical tasks foreseen during the first semester. Likewise, all partners have contributed to the communication strategy foreseen in the project by elaborating and publishing press releases and reviews in websites and social networks related to the project start-up and the development of the specific activities of each partner. Additionally, the consortium has carried out the planned cost declaration of expenses, including the necessary technical reports.
During the first year, the project activities were developed as planned, making progresses in the work towards the achievement of the established objectives, contributing to the creation of a sustainable tourism model in the GIAHS territories and in the establishment of the bases for the consolidation of a network in the Mediterranean area.

The following events, key to the development and progress of the project, were held:

The consortium started its activities with the first Steering Committee meeting and the 1st Interregional Seminar in March 2024. Both events were held on-line.

The 1st Steering Committee focused on the presentation of the partners, discussed administrative and financial aspects, approved the financial and governance rules of the MED-GIAHS project and established criteria for the monitoring and communication of the project.

At the 1st Interregional Seminar, the methodological basis of the project was established. At this event, the following aspects were presented:
-	The VALSIPAM strategy,
-	Methodology, schedule and work plan for the MED-GHIAS Strategy for sustainable agricultural tourism
-	The methodology, schedule and work plan for the Joint Guide for defining action plans.
-	The methodology, schedule and work plan for the Joint Training Plan (JTP).
-	The methodology, schedule and work plan for the MED-GHIAS sustainable tourism catalogues.

In May 2024, the 1st Online Technical Meeting was held with the participation of the technicians designated by each partner. There, the following topics were discussed and instructions were given for the development of the first local workshop:
-	Criteria and main features of a GIAHS site. Tourism Strategies within action plans
-	MED GIAHS site sustainable tourism strategy: 1st step and local workplan
-	MED GIAHS site sustainable tourism catalogues: methodology

The Lead Partner of the Community4Tourism (C4T) project also participated in this session, explaining the relationship with the sustainable tourism community. Another on-line meeting was held with this project in November.

Another key milestone of this period has been the work done on the adaptation of the VALSIPAM strategy, which has been improved with experiences from other projects. For its adaptation, the 1st Local Workshops were held in the six territories, where the agro-tourism resources were identified with the participation of key actors. In parallel, information was collected through surveys and workshops, consolidating a database for the tourism analysis of the GIAHS sites. Six analyses have been carried out, one for each territory, in the following territories:

-	Katuns of Bjelasica, Komovi, and Prokletije (Montenegro)
-	Historical Irrigation System at l'Horta de València (Spain)
-	Olive Groves of the Slopes between Assisi and Spoleto (Italy)
-	Bulgarian Rose agroecosystem (Bulgaria)
-	Gum Mastiha of Chios Island (Greece)
-	Malaga Raisin Production System in La Axarquía (Spain)

Based on these 6 analyses, a joint analysis of good practices was carried out.

Subsequently, in October 2024, the 2nd Steering Committee, the 2nd Interregional Seminar and the 1st Study Visit to the GIAHS site of the Huerta de Valencia were organised in Valencia, with the participation of all the partners and key actors.

The 2nd Steering Committee addressed the administrative, economic, and communication management of the project, as well as the methodology for calculating the carbon footprint. The activity schedule was reviewed, and adjustments were reported to the EUROMED Programme without affecting the overall timeline. Partners agreed to request the inclusion of a new training-of-trainers activity to ensure the preparation of local experts without altering the budget. The project’s Advisory Board was also established.

The 2nd Interregional Seminar served to review progress and present the first drafts of the MED-GIAHS Strategy for Sustainable Agricultural Tourism, the Joint Guide for Action Plans, and the Joint Training Plan. It highlighted the importance of the active involvement of partners in collecting and disseminating communication actions, including both their own initiatives and those of other partners and entities in their territories. The event was attended by FAO-recognized partners from North Africa, other Spanish GIAHS sites, and representatives from partner countries.

In communication and dissemination, a working group was created, composed of one representative per partner and coordinated by the project’s communication manager. CAPADR, as communication coordinator, hired a specialized communication company to support the consortium. Key developments in this area, with contributions from all partners, include: the development of the Communication Plan, the design and exhibition of the project poster at official venues, content creation and website updates, production of a project presentation video, and publication of news on both partner and official project websites.
During the third implementation period, the MED-GIAHS project made significant progress in consolidating its objectives, both in terms of technical activities and project coordination, in line with the established work plan.

The work focused on the preparation of the four strategic documents foreseen as final deliverables of the project. Their status is as follows:

The final versions of the Joint Guide for Defining Action Plans (D.2.1.1) and the MED-GIAHS Strategy for Sustainable Agricultural Tourism (D.3.1.1) were produced. Both documents were coordinated by CHdV, the partner responsible for drafting them, with the support of CAPADR and the rest of the partners, and were reviewed and validated by the Advisory Board at the time of this report (September 2025).

The final version of the Joint Training Plan/Pedagogical Guide (D.2.2.1) was also completed, drafted by UNIFI-DAGRI with the support of CAPADR and the other partners. The definitive deliverable will include the results of the training actions (Training for Trainers and Training for Stakeholders) and will be presented at the project’s final events, scheduled to take place in the Assisi and Spoleto GIAHS site between 20 and 22 November.

Considerable progress was also made on the MED-GIAHS Sustainable Tourism Catalogue (D.3.1.1), led by CAPADR. Within this framework, all local co-creation workshops were held across the project territories, engaging local stakeholders in the design of agrotourism experiences. In the last semester, the experiences to be included in the transnational catalogue will be selected, which will constitute the final deliverable.

Progress was also achieved in the establishment of the MED-GIAHS Network, both in terms of content (the four project deliverables) and in the definition of protocols and governance mechanisms. Its launch is scheduled for the final events in November.

Regarding specific activities, special mention should be made of the training actions led by UNIFI-DAGRI. As of this report (September 2025), the Training for Trainers sessions have been held in all territories except Montenegro (scheduled for the week of 6–10 October). With respect to the Training for Stakeholders, this activity has been completed by two partners (PP5 UNIFI and PP6 PRA) and is partially implemented in the others, following the planned schedule.

In terms of coordination and governance, CAPADR organized the 2nd online Technical Meeting (26/02/2025) and the 3rd Steering Committee Meeting (15/05/2025), also held online. These meetings addressed technical and administrative issues, as well as substantive matters such as the withdrawal of a partner (PP4 CHdV), clarification on State aid, the project modification, and the preparation of the events planned in La Axarquía GIAHS site (Málaga) in September 2025. Quality control, project monitoring and risk management mechanisms were also reinforced.

Following the dissolution of partner PP4 CHdV, the project was modified. The modification consisted of a reduction in partnership (withdrawal of one partner) and the redistribution of the remaining budget among the others (except CAPADR, whose budget remained unchanged). This redistribution will allow the implementation of three new communication and dissemination activities:

- Participation in the FAO 1st World Fair in Rome (10–13 October 2025).
- Production of a promotional video on each partner’s agricultural heritage system.
- Acquisition of goodies from some of the agricultural heritage systems involved in the project for exhibition and distribution as giveaways at the FAO Fair.

The project’s communication strategy was reinforced not only with these new activities, but also with the launch of social media accounts, the update of the website, and the production of graphic material (roll-up, photocall, posters, brochures). In addition, the second local co-creation workshops and the training activities were widely disseminated, showing high participation and satisfaction levels.

Regarding coordination with the Mission’s Governance Projects (TCP & IDP) and the JS, the project participated in the online meetings convened (February), the Second MED Cluster on Climate & Biodiversity (April), and the Dialogue for Tourism (March). Attendance is also planned at the Sustainable Tourism Community Annual Meeting, to be held in Tirana on 1–2 October.

Partners remain committed to carbon footprint measurement tasks, progressing in data collection and analysis, while simultaneously promoting actions aimed at its gradual and coordinated reduction.

Finally, communication with the JS has been continuous and fluid, enabling correct and safe progress in project activities.

en.name: Developing community based innovative business models for the revival of the internal areas in the Mediterranean
en.description:
In the last decades, the internal territories of the Mediterranean have been affected by steady depopulation processes that have led people to move to urban and coastal areas with a higher concentration of economic activities. Low economic dynamism, scarce public services, lack of job opportunities, poor digitalization and accessibility are the main challenges that make the internal areas more vulnerable.

REVIVE fits into this context, by building on participatory and capacity building approaches to reinforce the collaboration among quadruple helix stakeholders, strengthen the cohesion of communities and co-design innovative and integrated solutions to common challenges.

Through a transnational cooperation and coordination project partners will develop and test collaborative economic models through the creation of Innovative Community Cooperatives with the aim of developing interconnected products and services for the supply chains of existing economic sectors. In parallel, the project will co-design a set of digital services and test the application of advanced technologies through the creation of Mediterranean Digital Innovation Hubs. The MDIHs will then be integrated in the REVIVE transnational cluster of Innovative Community Cooperatives that will jointly provide concrete solutions for creating new business opportunities, encourage people to live and invest in these areas. 

In it's final phase the project will provide a transferring framework that will set the basis for up-scaling of results, strengthen the cohesion and promote the transition towards competitive, innovative and resilient ecosystems of the internal territories of the transnational cooperation area.
en.expected_achievements:
Define a methodological framework for developing community based collaborative economy business models and enabling innovation processes in the Mediterranean internal areas.
Enabling the creation of the REVIVE cluster of  Innovative Community Cooperatives by testing of new collaborative business models  in project  pilot areas
To enhance digital skills needed for uptaking of  advanced technologies and integrate the Mediterranean Digital Innovation Hubs in the REVIVE Cluster of ICCs  
To ensure the sustainability and transferability of the project results by contributing to the Result amplification strategy and by delivering of the transferring framework.
en.actual_achievements:
The first phase of the project, was focused on defining a methodological framework for developing community based collaborative economy business models and enabling innovation processes in our pilot areas. The work started with a deep analysis of the pilot areas to understand the current situation, the challenges and problems they face and to identify the opportunities for collaboration and innovation. 

Based on a template provided by IPTPO (activity leader) a deep analysis of the pilot areas was done with information on the existing policies at the national/ regional level and on the characteristics of the pilot areas. A detailed mapping and description of the policies on rural development, tourism, creative industries, innovation (RIS), social and employment and any other policies that could be consider relevant (sustainable growth, climate change, etc.) in addressing the challenges of our pilot areas, have been identified and analysed. Then a detailed analysis of each pilot areas was conducted based on the following: 1) demography characteristics, 2) climatic information, 3) natural conditions (e.g. forests, plains, landscape), 4) cultural heritage, 5) infrastructure and mobility, 6) internet infrastructure and digital services, 7) economic analysis and collaboration and connections of various entities), 8) SWOT analysis of the pilot areas. 

All this detailed information has been gathered in a final deliverable D1.1.1 that includes all the analysis of the pilot areas as well as a summary of the analysis with mind maps that provide an overview of existing strategies and policies at the national, regional and local levels divided into pilot areas and pertains to one of the following fields: rural development and agriculture, tourism, creative industry, innovation, society and employment, and others. Also, a brief overview of the characteristic economic activities for each area as well as the number of business entities and employees in the primary, secondary, and tertiary sectors. As a result this analysis, it provides insight into the common main features of the internal and external environment of the project's pilot areas. It is a document analysis that will help the pilot areas to develop a comprehensive action plans to implement an innovative business models in their areas and also work together based on the common challenges that they face.

In parallel, the activity of exploiting the lessons learnt by past and ongoing projects and best practises was also done by all partners aiming to raise awareness about innovative and collaborative business models, measures and projects, sharing the good practices implemented in different countries, strengthening peer-to-peer learning and innovative development. Following the instructions of e-Zavod based on a template, all partners collected best practices for developing innovative business models based on three main drivers, that is innovation process, digitalization and technology and collaborative economy and also based on economic sectors, such as agriculture, production and trade of local products, tourism and culture, energy and infrastructures and mixed sectors. Deliverable 1.2.1 was drafted and shared with the partners.

One of our main goals during the first period of the project was the engagement of the stakeholders from the beginning of the project. The process started with the identification of the relevant stakeholders and the organization of regional workshops in each pilot area to discuss the challenges, necessities and possible solutions that can be addressed in the pilot areas in the fields of agriculture, food production, tourism and cultural and creative industry. 

The results of the regional workshops, the analysis of the pilot areas and best practises and the framework for testing were detailed discussed during the meeting in Leros, Greece beginning of July before starting with the local pilot action plans process. We brough all the stakeholder's from the beginning together to exchange on their experiences, to get inspired from each other and to set the ground for future collaboration for the implementation of the pilot actions.  

The output O1.1 REVIVE Framework for pilot testing has been also reached since the final business model framework has been drafted that constitutes an operational instrument to favour innovation processes by promoting emergent community based on collaborative economy business models and uptake of advanced technologies. The model is unique for each area and it includes all the steps that guide pilot areas in defining a business model suitable for their area.

The social media (Facebook, LinkedIn, Instagram) and the project website have been set up. Until project website was ready, dissemination was done through the social media. We presented the pilot areas, participation to events and the organization of regional workshops. In total we have 20 posts. Also, our poster and banner have been designed.
The focus of the second semester of the project was on the establishment of the innovative community cooperatives in the 7 pilot areas as well as defining the potential digital services and advanced technologies to be implemented to the pilot areas in the next two periods. 

Partners, following the REVIVE business model framework developed on the first period as well as the set up of the stakeholder groups, established their innovation community cooperatives and drafted the relevant action plans that will be implemented in period 3 and 4. More specifically, at the beginning of the semester, the D2.1.1 Engagement and participatory process action plan (ANCI Toscana) was prepared to provide to the partners guidelines and a step by step process on how to engage and facilitate the participation of the local stakeholder groups, in different steps of co-design and implementation of the action plans in the 7 project pilot areas, leading to the establishment of the Innovative Community Cooperatives and the Digital Innovation Hubs and their subsequent merging into the transnational cluster of REVIVE communities.  Also, in the D2.1.1, the REVIVE Pilot action plan template was included as well as the stakeholder list template and engagement calendar template that was filled by all partners with their activities (meetings, workshops, etc).

All partners identified their local stakeholders and organize different meetings, workshops and events with the main aim to engage the local stakeholders and to work together to define the activities and draft the action plans. As a result, each pilot area has drafted its action plan (D2.2.1 - 7 Action Plans) that includes a brief description of the initial engagement process, a description of who is joining (or founding the innovation community cooperative), the economic sectors involved, the collaborative business model description, the activities, the monitoring and evaluation of the action plan as well as the economic sustainability and plans for the future.

Another important activity for the second period was the setting up of the MED Digital innovation hubs (MDIHs)  and
digital laboratories in the pilot areas. Under the guidance of MCC, all partners have identified the DIHubs in their areas and already had the first communication with them as to establish cooperation for the capacity building activities (trainings in digital technologies). Also, all partners identified the initiatives related to technology applied to the sectors of the pilot areas. Based on discussions with the local stakeholders on the digital technologies and immersive experiences the partners pilot areas have identified the technologies that will implemented in their pilot areas and would bring improvements. This activities are also included in the action plans. For the Data Integration the data platform will be based on FIWARE (NADIA platform from AnySolution) that enables the collection and analysis of data from heterogeneous sources and formats and also allows for an easy visualisation and interpretation of data through dashboards. This activity will be starting in the next semester.

The project website and social media have been updated on a regular basis. In this period we had more than 40 posts in our social media. The followers are as follow: Facebook 73, LinkedIn: 75 and Instagram: 36. All partners have contributed with news articles of their activities. Also, the project communication plan has been approved.

The 2nd project meeting and study visit was organized at the beginning of the period in Leros, Greece (10-11 July 2024) with the participation of local stakeholders. Τhe results of the regional workshops, the analysis of the pilot areas and best practises and the framework for testing were detailed discussed. The stakeholders had the opportunity to meet and  exchange on their experiences, to get inspired from each other and to set the ground for future collaboration for the implementation of the pilot actions.  

The 3rd project meeting was organized in Maribor, Slovenia (13-14/11/2024). The main aim of the meeting was the discussion of the next steps for the pilot areas action plans for the set up of the innovation community cooperatives and the implementation of the immersive experiences. Study visits were also organized for exchange of experience and inspiration for the partners.

The focus of the 3rd semester was on the implementation of the Action Plans of the 7 pilot areas focusing on the ICC and the development of the immersive experiences: 

ANETEL: engaging stakeholders with several meetings and especially with the core group for the implementation of the action plan (setting up the ICC and identify the branding and communication for rural Larnaca). The immersive experience to be developed was agreed which is the creation of an interactive AI Avatar that will welcome visitors and respond in real time to questions about the pilot area highlighting also existing immersive experiences of rural Larnaca.

CNR-ANCI: drafted the Charter of Values and analyse potential legal structures of the Food Community. They drafted the Statute and Articles of the Association (signed by 12 members) and registered officially the Pistoia Mountain Food Community. They design a logo and visual identity, develop an official website for the Food Community, create promotional materials and defined a targeted communication strategy. They customized training sessions on digital marketing and immersive technologies and started the process for the immersive experiences (360 VR Videos).

IPTPO: engage stakeholders with relevant meetings and discussions for creating a digital solution. They further encourage local participation by organising the event ‘Study visit Istria’, bring together project partners and their stakeholders. The 1st digital workshop was organised aiming  to involve stakeholders in the co-creation process of the b2b platform. Further, they collaborated with EDIH Adria and drafted the Digitalisation Action Plan. IPTPO started to design their digital solution.

MCC-AETIB: develop and assess the feasibility of the ideas contained in the Local Action. AETIB did benchmarking research on existing B2B platforms in the Balearic Islands used by hotel chains to purchase local foods and decided to support an already existing platform that promotes the commercialisation of local products among the tourist sectors. Also, they have assessed funding possibilities as well as the willingness of producers and tourist businesses in the pilot area to join the platform. They had several meetings (16) with stakeholders to ensure coordination, long-term sustainability, and adoption of the solution. Further, they advanced in the organization of the digital laboratories, by defining 4 training sessions and organized the 1st international course “Digital Innovation Hubs: an introduction” that took place on 2nd of July.
  
SWG-RRD: started promoting honey production, local products and natural and cultural heritage of the pilot area. Trainings and workshops were organized ion cooperation, LAGs, leadership, advantages and disadvantages of cooperatives. Also, they started to test the application of drones in agriculture by mapping  and prescription map creation to showcase the potential use in agriculture activities. Also, they decided to develop a prototype for monitoring the behavior of the bees in the beehive.

e-Zavod: started the implementation of the Local Action Plan for the area of Slovenske gorice through a participatory co-design process involving local stakeholders. They started the process for the establishment of the Slovenske Gorice Experience Cooperative (SGX Coop), a community-led platform aiming to develop sustainable and experiential tourism, promote digital innovation, and strengthen local entrepreneurship.  Also, they launched a Digital Innovation Hub and operationalised a digital lab in Slovenske gorice. Five tailored training modules were designed to be implemented through national workshops.

READ SA.: supported the continuation of the Aegean Cuisine network that  enters a new era of digital communication, strengthening its brand and boosting the gastronomic and cultural profile of the Dodecanese.

The project website and social media have been updated on a regular basis. In this period, we had more than 30 posts in our social media. The followers are as follow: Facebook 101, LinkedIn: 113 and Instagram: 53. All partners have contributed with news articles of their activities. Two transnational exchanges were organized. The first in Porec, Istria (18-19/2/2025) and the second in Appennino Pistoiese (Tuscany) on 19-21/3/2025. The transnational exchanges were an opportunity for partners and their stakeholders to meet, exchange and see best practises from the pilot areas. The 4th project meeting was organized in Skopjie (25-26 /6/2025) discussing the setup of the innovation community cooperatives and the implementation of immersive experiences. Study visits was also organized for exchange of experience and inspiration for the partners. Last, we participated to all the activities of the ISE Mission. We are participating to the mentoring programme where  REVIVE is a giver to AgroID Social Cooperative Enterprise: focused on empowering small-scale farmers.

fr.name: Developing community based innovative business models for the revival of the internal areas in the Mediterranean
fr.description:
Au cours des dernières décennies, les territoires internes de la Méditerranée ont été affectés par un processus continu de dépeuplement, qui a conduit les habitants à se déplacer vers des zones urbaines et côtières avec une plus grande concentration d'activités économiques. 
Les principaux défis qui rendent ces zones plus vulnérables sont un faible dynamisme économique, des services publics médiocres, le manque d'opportunités d'emploi, une numérisation et une accessibilité insuffisantes.
REVIVE s'inséré dans ce contexte grâce à des approches participatives et de renforcement des capacités, pour améliorer la collaboration entre les partenaires soutenir la cohésion communautaire et concevoir ensemble des solutions aux défis communs qui soient innovantes et intégrées. 
Grace à un projet transnational de coopération et de coordination, les partenaires développeront et testeront des modèles économiques collaboratifs, qui prévoient la création de coopératives communautaires innovantes, dans le but de développer des produits et services interconnectés pour les chaînes d'approvisionnement des secteurs économiques existants. 
En parallèle, le projet créera un ensemble de services numériques et testera l'application de technologies avancées, à travers la création de pôles méditerranéens d'innovation numérique. 
Les MDIH seront ensuite intégrés dans le cluster transnational REVIVE des coopératives communautaires innovantes, qui fourniront conjointement des solutions concrètes, pour créer des nouvelles opportunités d'affaires, encourager les populations à continuer vivre et à investir dans ces zones.
Le projet fournira, dans sa phase finale, un cadre de transfert qui posera les bases de l’expansion des résultats, renforcera la cohésion et favorisera la transition des territoires internes inclus dans l'espace de coopération transnationale, vers des écosystèmes compétitifs, innovants et résilients.

fr.name: Accelerating Carbon Market Development for climate change mitigation and adaptation in Mediterranean agriculture
fr.description:
Parmi les principaux défis identifiés dans la loi européenne sur le climat figure la neutralité climatique de l'ensemble du secteur foncier d'ici 2035. Pour atteindre cet objectif, les mesures à mettre en place doivent suivre une approche associant eau, énergie et sécurité alimentaire. Cette approche correspond aux principes de la gestion durable des terres et aux pratiques qui y contribuent en augmentant progressivement le captage à long terme du CO2 de l'atmosphèredans les écosystèmes. En outre, elle favorise la restauration de l'environnement et participe à une exploitation plus résiliente et équitable des terres par les communautés locales.
L'objectif général de CARBON FARMING MED est de développer un système agricole méditerranéen résilient et d'aider les agriculteurs à mettre en œuvre l'agriculture carbone comme un nouveau modèle de gestion écologique permettant d'obtenir des revenus supplémentaires, d'optimiser les pratiques agricoles et agroforestières régénératrices et de fournir les outils nécessaires à l'adoption d'un marché des crédits carbone (vendeur et acheteur) grâce à:


1.	La création d'un cadre théorique de l'agriculture carbone en Méditerranée


2.	La quantification de la capture du carbone dans le sol et des émissions de GES évitées


3.	La promotion d'un marché méditerranéen des crédits carbone générés par l'agriculture régénérative


4.	Le transfert des résultats du projet
Tout cela nécessite une approche transfrontalière réunissant acheteurs et vendeurs en Méditerranée pour qu'ils puissent tirer profit d'un marché commun de crédits carbone. CARBON FARMING MED sera la première plateforme de ce type dans la région qui permettra d'une part d'acheter et de vendre des crédits carbone et d'autre par d'aider les exploitations dans le processus de vérification en capitalisant sur les modèles et les bonnes pratiques existants, créant ainsi un cadre pour l'agriculture carbone en Méditerranée qui permettra à l'UE d'atteindre la neutralité climatique d'ici 2050.
fr.expected_achievements:
Adapting the current EU regulatory framework on carbon farming to the Mediterranean idiosyncrasies. Aims to define the potential carbon sequestration, the monitoring and reporting initiatives and to assess the business potential of carbon farming.
The WP will validate the most promising agroforestry and regenerative agriculture actions in relation to its carbon sequestration potential aiming to mitigate farmer’s risk and to promote wider adoption of new carbon farming actions in their farms.
The WP will provide to the involved stakeholders (e.g., farmers) the tools (e.g., MRV web applications, a seller/buyer meeting point, etc.) to promote an internal market of carbon credits from Mediterranean carbon farming initiatives.  
Transfer and mainstreaming of the project results and lessons learned, through capitalisation of the project developments and results.

en.name: Accelerating Carbon Market Development for climate change mitigation and adaptation in Mediterranean agriculture
en.description:
Among the main challenges identified in the European Climate Law is achieving climate neutrality of the entire land sector by 2035. Effective means to reach this target follow a water-energy-food security nexus approach. This is also in line with the principles of sustainable land management, and practices that are responding to the challenge by helping to gradually increase the capture of CO2 from the atmosphere and store it in the long term in ecosystems. Those practices also foster the restoration of the environment and contribute to a more resilient and fair exploitation of the land for local communities.
The general objective of CARBON FARMING MED is to develop a resilient Mediterranean agriculture system and to help farmers to implement carbon farming as a new green business model to obtain additional revenues (e.g., voluntary market of carbon credits, CAP funding, etc.), optimizing regenerative agricultural and agroforestry practices and providing the necessary tools to facilitate the carbon credits’ market adoption (from seller and buyer perspectives), through:



1.	Establishing a theoretical framework of carbon farming in the Mediterranean


2.	Quantifying carbon capture in the soil and avoided GHG emissions


3.	Promoting a Mediterranean market of carbon credits generated by regenerative agriculture


4.	Transferring and mainstreaming the project results and lessons learned

The above requires a cross-border approach that brings Mediterranean carbon buyers and sellers together to benefit from a common market of carbon credits. The CARBON FARMING MED will be the first platform of its kind in the region, allowing to buy and sell carbon credits, while offering a tool that helps farms with the verification process, by capitalising on existing models and best practices and creating a carbon farming framework in the Mediterranean. This will tackle the joint challenge of enabling the EU to achieve climate neutrality by 2050, through carbon removals.

Adapting the current EU regulatory framework on carbon farming to the Mediterranean idiosyncrasies. Aims to define the potential carbon sequestration, the monitoring and reporting initiatives and to assess the business potential of carbon farming.
The WP will validate the most promising agroforestry and regenerative agriculture actions in relation to its carbon sequestration potential aiming to mitigate farmer’s risk and to promote wider adoption of new carbon farming actions in their farms.
The WP will provide to the involved stakeholders (e.g., farmers) the tools (e.g., MRV web applications, a seller/buyer meeting point, etc.) to promote an internal market of carbon credits from Mediterranean carbon farming initiatives.  
Transfer and mainstreaming of the project results and lessons learned, through capitalisation of the project developments and results.
en.actual_achievements:
During the first reporting period, all consortium members actively participated in meetings organized by the coordination team, including the first consortium meeting (February 1st) and the KoM developed in Vic, Spain (February 19th - March 1st). Additionally, members took part in key coordination events such as the ISE MED Innovation Day in France (June 2024), the 7th EURAF Congress in the Czech Republic (May 2024), and the 1st European Carbon Farming Summit in Spain (March 2024). A total of 32 technical meetings were also held between consortium members, either coordinated by the lead or requested by partners (see project calendar on Basecamp).
The presentation of the Carbon Farming Projects Catalogue (D4.1, June 2024) has been a key resource for establishing connections with other ongoing European-funded projects, (It was developed a bilateral meeting with Life Carbon Farming project, led by the Institut de l'Elevage, France- April 2024). It has also helped to develop a shortlist institutions and project coordinators for conforming the Advisory Board (invitations sent). The project's communication strategy, developed by IHE in close collaboration with EMP (June 2024, see WP4 on Basecamp), serves as a guide to enhance communication efficiency and stakeholder engagement. Additionally, the official website and social media accounts (Facebook, LinkedIn, Instagram) were launched in June 2024 to expand the project's visibility and encourage community involvement. The open call inviting Spanish and Italian farmers to join the project's pilot farms was published on the project’s website and social media channels (July 2024). This resulted in significant interest from both countries. Over the next 6-7 months, the selection process will be conducted to identify farms that meet the call's eligibility criteria. Following this, agreements will be signed, and technical recognition visits will commence. Finally, the Carbon Footprint Assessment and Offsetting Guidelines (D.4.8.1) were developed by CENER 21 to assist partners in understanding and utilizing the ZEROCO2Med tool (Activity 4.8 - Carbon Footprint Assessment and Offsetting). A meeting was held with the entire consortium to explain the process of data collection for each reporting period, ensuring all members are aligned on the methodology.

In WP1, the following progress was made: UVIC completed the Report on Carbon Farming as a Business Model (A1.1 and D1.1), which was reviewed, approved by partners, and submitted to the JS. IHE began A1.2 in month 3, analyzing MRV requirements and standards, forming the basis for further research. CREA started A1.3 in month 1, with the report (D1.3 – June 2024) expected by the second period's end. CREA also initiated A1.4, developing a SOC estimation model for the Mediterranean region's agroclimatic conditions.
WP2 focused on defining criteria for regenerative agriculture and agroforestry practices. Led by AZOLLA (A2.1) with support from CREA, TUC, EURAF, UVIC, and IHE, the D2.1 document (June 2024) was developed, forming the basis for the open call. CREA and AZOLLA actively encouraged local farmer participation, while IHE led a coordination meeting to synchronize efforts The key activities of WP3, led by UVIC, included the A3.1 report on the "Definition of Functionalities and Requirements to Develop the Carbon Farming MED Tool" (D3.1 – June 2024). Several technical meetings with partners were conducted to identify the minimum technical requirements for the MRV system and to define the core functionalities of the tool. Activity 3.2 commenced in month 4, though additional technical meetings are planned to finalize the frontend programming, aiming to prevent significant changes in the upcoming months.
In WP A4.1, led by EUROREGIO, the focus was on identifying existing European carbon farming projects to leverage available data and foster synergies. The European Carbon Farming Projects Catalogue (D4.1 - June 2024) was presented and thoroughly reviewed by the partners involved (IHE, CENER 21, CRITT, UVIC, AZOLLA). 

Learning pathways and potential collaborations were identified, including the recommendation to involve leaders from similar projects in the advisory board. In A4.4, IHE drafted the communication strategy with significant input from Euroregio, which also contributed to the development of communication tools such as the project poster, institutional website, and project website. Under A4.8, CENER 21 developed an Excel tool for carbon footprint assessment and provided training on its use, with all partners completing the file to test the tool's functionality.

During the second reporting period, the Carbon Farming MED project made substantial progress across all work packages, focusing on research, pilot testing, digital platform development, communication, and project management.
WP1
Significant efforts advanced research on Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification (MRV) standards at global, EU, and national levels. A review of CO₂ offsetting platforms assessed real-world MRV applications and challenges. These findings form the basis for a tailored MRV framework adapted to Mediterranean carbon farming practices in the next phase.
Carbon farming practice selection identified agroforestry and regenerative agriculture as the most suitable approaches for the region. Predictive soil sequestration models were adapted to assess carbon storage potential under Mediterranean conditions. A Soil Sampling Protocol was developed to ensure data collection consistency across pilot farms, alongside testing RothC soil carbon sequestration models.
A preliminary review of techno-economic models for agro-system performance led to drafting the methodology for Activity A1.5. Collaboration strengthened research integration, aligning with project goals. Deliverable D3.1: Selection of Best-Suited Carbon Farming Actions was successfully completed. To enhance Output 1.1, a proposal was approved to postpone delivery to the next period, ensuring it fully incorporates WP1 findings and Mediterranean stakeholder needs.
WP2
Fieldwork intensified, assessing and implementing pilot testing activities across multiple Mediterranean sites. Site visits in Spain and Italy evaluated farm characteristics, land access, and production types, providing the foundation for selecting sampling plots and standardizing field data collection. From October to December 2024, systematic soil sampling followed Chronosequence and Contrasting Land Use approaches. Samples are undergoing laboratory analysis to determine carbon content and sequestration potential.
Pilot farms implementing agroforestry and regenerative agriculture were monitored for carbon sequestration performance and economic feasibility. A combination of modeling and direct measurement techniques assessed their impact, contributing to project objectives.
WP3
A major milestone was the continued development of the Carbon Farming MED digital platform, a key tool for farmers, researchers, and policymakers. Work advanced on frontend and backend functionalities, integrating complex data processing with a user-friendly interface.
A prototype user interface (MOOC in Figma) was designed to collect farmer data on Soil Organic Carbon (SOC) and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The platform's architecture supports carbon removal project development aligned with certification schemes like Verra and the International Carbon Registry (ICR).
Backend development established a secure login and registration system. Preliminary soil carbon accumulation modeling using the CAST model yielded initial results, presented at the Soil Health: Current Status and Future Needs conference in Chania, Greece.
WP4
Efforts focused on strengthening visibility, fostering stakeholder engagement, and ensuring smooth administration. A key achievement was establishing the Advisory Board (AB), with its first formal meeting scheduled for January 2025, bringing together external members, associated partners, and the steering committee.
A Framework for Effective Project Communication was developed to streamline information sharing. Communication materials included a project roll-up and a promotional video (pending approval). The project actively participated in national, European, and Mediterranean-level events, including:
-	1st European Carbon Farming Summit (Valencia, Spain) – presenting to a European audience.
-	Simposio Internacional del Carbono en México (Online) – presenting to North American institutions.
-	CONAMA (National Congress of Environment, Spain) – presenting to a national audience.
-	ISE Community of Practice Gathering & MedTalks – engaging in knowledge-sharing discussions.
Monthly technical and coordination meetings ensured partner alignment and Interreg project adherence. The Carbon Farming Projects Catalogue was expanded, identifying new initiatives and engaging key stakeholders for advisory board participation.
Project sustainability efforts progressed in carbon footprint assessment, with data collection and submission compliant with reporting guidelines. Preparations for the EuroMED Academy & Community Activities began, with an initial work plan and content gathering.

Request to delay Output O.1.1
Regarding O.1.1, after reviewing project progress, it was determined that delivering this output in Period 3, after WP1 completion, would be more appropriate. This adjustment ensures the framework incorporates consolidated results, enhancing quality and applicability. The Project Officer was officially informed on 30/01/2025.
.


During the third reporting period, the Carbon Farming Med project advanced significantly in administrative, technical, and dissemination activities. The consortium successfully postponed the delivery of Output 1.1 to June 2025 to ensure integration of all WP1 results.
On the technical side, WP1 advanced the Carbon Farming Framework, delivering progress on documents, models, and databases: a conventional agriculture baseline scenario, statistical tools in R, and a database of over 100 studies assessing carbon farming externalities. WP2 completed soil analyses and advanced farm diagnostics across Spain and Italy, developing a standardized farmer description form to harmonize data collection. WP3 progressed with the integration of Roth C into the IT platform, including tools for farm polygon uploads and interface development. WP4 strengthened dissemination, expanding LinkedIn outreach, producing educational content, and initiating synergies with projects such as Carbonica, LILAS4Soil, and IRISCC.
The first Advisory Board meeting provided constructive feedback and fostered synergies. The consortium also prepared contributions to major events (EU Carbon Farming Summit, Bioeconomy Congress, Eurosoil 2025), strengthening policy links and Mediterranean-wide collaboration.
